_meta.description: Curated time-axis network of human knowledge: ~300 nodes spanning the emergence of language to the research frontiers of 2026. Edges encode three relationships: enables (foundational dependency), influences (conceptual borrowing), refines (succession or replacement).

schemaNotes.date: Signed integer year, negative = BCE. ISO 8601 string for sub-year precision in modern era.
schemaNotes.dateUncertainty: Plus/minus in years. Large for prehistoric events.
schemaNotes.importance: 1-5 integer; drives node size and visibility under the importance filter.
schemaNotes.frontier: true if the event opens an active research area as of 2026 (dashed border + glow).
schemaNotes.edges.type: One of: enables (foundational dependency), influences (conceptual borrowing), refines (succession/replacement).

_meta.curationGuidance: This expansion prioritizes the under-represented domains called out in the seed: medicine, earth sciences, social sciences, and language/communication, plus deeper non-Western coverage (Chinese astronomy/medicine/technology, Indian linguistics and mathematics, Islamic Golden Age scholarship, Mesoamerican mathematics, African metallurgy and tallies). To add events, append to nodes[] and reference ids in edges[] - see README.

nodes.label: Emergence of symbolic language
nodes.description: Development of fully recursive, symbolic spoken language in Homo sapiens. Enabled abstract reasoning, social coordination, and cross-generational cultural transmission - the cognitive substrate beneath every later intellectual achievement.

nodes.label: Cave art and symbolic representation
nodes.description: Figurative and symbolic visual art at Chauvet, Lascaux, Sulawesi, and elsewhere. The first durable externalization of mental imagery; a precondition for later notation systems.

nodes.label: Agricultural revolution
nodes.description: Independent domestication of plants and animals in the Fertile Crescent, Yangtze, Mesoamerica, and elsewhere. Produced food surpluses, sedentary populations, and the demographic pressure that drove cities, specialization, and record-keeping.

nodes.label: Cuneiform writing
nodes.description: The first system to encode language in durable, decodable form, developed in Sumer for administrative record-keeping. Transformed knowledge from purely oral transmission into a portable, accumulable medium.

nodes.label: Euclidean geometry
nodes.description: Euclid's Elements systematized Greek geometric knowledge into an axiomatic deductive framework. Set the template - definitions, axioms, theorems, proofs - that all later mathematics and theoretical science would follow.

nodes.label: Aristotelian logic
nodes.description: Aristotle's Organon formalized syllogistic reasoning and categorical logic. Dominated Western thought on valid inference for nearly two millennia and remained the foundation of philosophy and science through the early modern era.

nodes.label: Hippocratic medicine
nodes.description: The Hippocratic corpus framed disease as natural rather than divine and grounded medicine in observation, case histories, and ethical practice. Established the conceptual posture of clinical medicine as a rational discipline.

nodes.label: Paper-making in Han China
nodes.description: Cai Lun's process for making paper from plant fibers. A cheap, light, durable writing surface that - once it diffused through the Islamic world and eventually Europe - multiplied the scale at which texts could be produced and shared.

nodes.label: Zero as a number
nodes.description: Brahmagupta's Brahmasphutasiddhanta gave the first formal treatment of zero as a number with arithmetic rules, building on earlier Indian use of zero as a positional placeholder. Made the place-value decimal system fully operative.

nodes.label: Al-Khwarizmi's algebra
nodes.description: Al-Khwarizmi's Al-Jabr introduced systematic methods for solving linear and quadratic equations and gave algebra its name. Together with translated Greek mathematics, it formed the core curriculum that re-entered Europe through Andalusia.

nodes.label: Ibn al-Haytham's Book of Optics
nodes.description: The Kitab al-Manazir advanced a theory of vision based on light entering the eye and pioneered controlled experimentation with explicit hypotheses. An early articulation of empirical method that influenced later Islamic and European natural philosophy.

nodes.label: Gutenberg's movable-type press
nodes.description: Gutenberg's metal movable-type press in Mainz - building on earlier Chinese and Korean precedents - collapsed the cost of reproducing text. Drove mass literacy, the Reformation, and the rapid circulation of scientific work.

nodes.label: Copernican heliocentrism
nodes.description: De revolutionibus orbium coelestium placed the Sun, not the Earth, at the center of the planetary system. Dethroned the human observer from cosmic privilege and forced later astronomers and physicists to build a new mechanics.

nodes.label: Empirical scientific method
nodes.description: Bacon's Novum Organum proposed inductive reasoning from controlled experience as the engine of new knowledge - explicitly against Aristotelian deduction. Galileo's experimental practice gave it teeth. Codified the methodological signature of modern science.

nodes.label: Differential and integral calculus
nodes.description: Independent invention by Newton (fluxions) and Leibniz (differentials) of a language for instantaneous change and accumulation. Became the indispensable tool of every physical science that followed.

nodes.label: Newtonian mechanics (Principia)
nodes.description: Newton's Philosophiae Naturalis Principia Mathematica unified terrestrial and celestial motion under three laws and universal gravitation. The first complete mathematical physics - the model of theoretical science for two centuries.

nodes.label: Wealth of Nations
nodes.description: Adam Smith's An Inquiry into the Nature and Causes of the Wealth of Nations introduced division of labor, the price mechanism, and the metaphor of the invisible hand. Founded economics as a systematic discipline.

nodes.label: Lavoisier's chemistry
nodes.description: Lavoisier's Traite elementaire de chimie established conservation of mass, identified oxygen's role in combustion, and gave chemistry a rational nomenclature. The break from alchemy to modern chemistry.

nodes.label: Darwinian evolution
nodes.description: On the Origin of Species presented natural selection as the mechanism by which species diversify. Unified all of biology under a single explanatory framework and decentered humanity from creation.

nodes.label: Maxwell's electromagnetism
nodes.description: Maxwell's equations unified electricity, magnetism, and light as manifestations of a single electromagnetic field. The first field theory and the first hint that classical mechanics was not the whole story.

nodes.label: Periodic table of elements
nodes.description: Mendeleev arranged the known elements by atomic weight into recurring chemical families, leaving gaps that predicted then-unknown elements. Made chemistry systematic and pointed toward an underlying atomic structure.

nodes.label: Germ theory of disease
nodes.description: Work by Pasteur, Koch, and Lister established that specific microorganisms cause specific diseases. Transformed surgery, public health, and the practice of medicine more than any prior idea.

nodes.label: General relativity
nodes.description: Einstein recast gravity as the curvature of spacetime by mass-energy, superseding Newton's action-at-a-distance description. Predicted gravitational lensing, black holes, and cosmic expansion.

nodes.label: Quantum mechanics
nodes.description: The Heisenberg-Schrodinger-Born-Dirac formulation of microscopic physics replaced classical determinism with probability amplitudes. Underpins chemistry, solid-state physics, and every modern electronic device.

nodes.label: Turing machine and computability
nodes.description: Turing's 'On Computable Numbers' defined a universal model of mechanical computation and proved the existence of uncomputable problems. The conceptual blueprint of every later computer.

nodes.label: The transistor
nodes.description: Bardeen, Brattain, and Shockley's solid-state amplifier at Bell Labs replaced the vacuum tube. The physical substrate for digital electronics, exponential scaling, and the entire information age.

nodes.label: Information theory
nodes.description: Shannon's 'A Mathematical Theory of Communication' defined entropy, channel capacity, and the bit. Created the quantitative foundation for compression, error correction, and all digital communication.

nodes.label: Structure of DNA
nodes.description: Watson, Crick, and Franklin's double-helix model showed how genetic information is physically stored and copied. Reduced inheritance to molecular chemistry and opened the modern era of molecular biology.

nodes.label: Plate tectonics
nodes.description: Synthesis of seafloor spreading, paleomagnetism, and continental drift into a single theory of mobile lithospheric plates. Did for earth science what evolution did for biology - unified its disparate observations under one mechanism.

nodes.label: ARPANET and the Internet
nodes.description: The first packet-switched wide-area network, connecting US universities and labs. Established the protocols and conventions (TCP/IP arrived 1983) that scaled into the global internet.

nodes.label: Deep learning revolution (AlexNet)
nodes.description: Krizhevsky, Sutskever, and Hinton's GPU-trained convolutional network won ImageNet by a wide margin, triggering the modern deep learning era. Re-centered AI research around large, learned representations.

nodes.label: CRISPR-Cas9 genome editing
nodes.description: Doudna and Charpentier's adaptation of the bacterial CRISPR system into a programmable genome-editing tool. Made precise, cheap editing of any genome routine and reset the agenda of molecular biology and medicine.

nodes.label: Transformer architecture and foundation models
nodes.description: Vaswani et al.'s 'Attention Is All You Need' introduced the Transformer, which scaled into the large language and multimodal models defining current AI. Active research frontier as of 2026.

nodes.label: Lebombo bone tally
nodes.description: A Swazi baboon fibula incised with 29 notches, plausibly a lunar tally. Among the oldest surviving evidence of intentional numerical record-keeping anywhere on Earth.

nodes.label: Blombos abstract engravings
nodes.description: Cross-hatched ochre patterns from Blombos Cave, South Africa, alongside shell-bead jewelry. Among the earliest unambiguous traces of abstract symbolic behavior in modern humans.

nodes.label: Ishango bone
nodes.description: A Central African baboon fibula bearing structured groups of notches, often read as an arithmetic or lunar table. An early hint that quantitative thought predates writing by tens of millennia.

nodes.label: Domestication of the dog
nodes.description: Wolves and humans co-adapted into the first interspecies partnership. A reproducible demonstration that artificial selection can reshape an organism within a few hundred generations.

nodes.label: Earliest fired pottery
nodes.description: Hunter-gatherer pottery in southern China, then independently elsewhere. The first deliberate transformation of one material (clay) into another (ceramic) through controlled high-temperature processing.

nodes.label: Gobekli Tepe monumental architecture
nodes.description: Massive stone enclosures in Anatolia, built by pre-agricultural communities. Forced a rethinking of the standard story that monumental construction requires settled farming and surplus.

nodes.label: Walls and tower of Jericho
nodes.description: The earliest known stone fortifications, surrounding a Neolithic town in the Jordan Valley. Evidence of settled, hierarchical communities able to organize labor on civic projects.

nodes.label: Copper smelting
nodes.description: Reduction of malachite ore to metallic copper, in the Balkans, the Near East, and independently elsewhere. The first deliberate chemical transformation - matter changed in kind, not just shape.

nodes.label: Irrigation agriculture
nodes.description: Canal networks tapping the Tigris and Euphrates - and contemporaneous systems on the Nile, Yellow, and Indus rivers. The first large-scale engineering of landscapes for productive surplus.

nodes.label: Bronze metallurgy
nodes.description: Alloying copper with tin (or arsenic) yielded a tougher, easier-cast metal that defined an era of toolmaking and trade across Eurasia. The first widely diffused composite material.

nodes.label: The wheel for transport
nodes.description: Wheeled vehicles emerged across the Pontic-Caspian steppe and Mesopotamia. A late innovation - relative to fire and weaving - because wheels demand smooth ground, axles, and lubrication to be useful.

nodes.label: Sericulture in China
nodes.description: Cultivation of Bombyx mori silkworms and the unwinding of cocoons into thread, kept a state secret for millennia. The Silk Road later carried not only fabric but the artifacts and ideas it bought.

nodes.label: Egyptian hieroglyphic writing
nodes.description: A logo-phonetic script that emerged in Egypt nearly contemporaneously with cuneiform. Combined word-signs, phonetic complements, and determinatives in an aesthetic continuous with monumental art.

nodes.label: Indus Valley script
nodes.description: Short inscriptions on seals and pottery from Harappan cities, still undeciphered. Evidence of a literate Bronze Age civilization whose linguistic and cultural ties remain unresolved.

nodes.label: Proto-Sinaitic alphabet
nodes.description: Semitic-speaking workers in the Sinai adapted Egyptian hieroglyphs into an acrophonic consonantal script. The probable common ancestor of Phoenician, Aramaic, Greek, Latin, Arabic, and Hebrew letters.

nodes.label: Pyramids of Giza
nodes.description: Old Kingdom monumental tombs requiring precise surveying, quarrying, transport, and stone placement at unprecedented scale. The defining demonstration that organized states could marshal labor and engineering knowledge across decades.

nodes.label: Indus Valley urban drainage
nodes.description: Mohenjo-daro and Harappa featured grid plans, standardized brickwork, and covered municipal sewers - public-health engineering more than four millennia before its rediscovery in modern Europe.

nodes.label: Iron smelting
nodes.description: Bloomery iron production spread from Anatolia and West Africa to most of Eurasia. Far more abundant than tin and copper, iron made tools and weapons accessible beyond elite use.

nodes.label: Phoenician alphabet
nodes.description: A 22-letter consonantal script carried by Phoenician merchants across the Mediterranean. Direct ancestor of Greek, Aramaic, and Hebrew writing.

nodes.label: Shang oracle bone script
nodes.description: Divinatory inscriptions on ox scapulae and turtle plastrons in Bronze Age China. The earliest fully attested ancestor of modern Chinese characters, and the founding archive of Chinese historiography.

nodes.label: Olmec/Cascajal proto-writing
nodes.description: Inscribed symbols from Veracruz, possibly the earliest writing in the Americas. Initiates the Mesoamerican literate tradition that flowers in Maya glyphs.

nodes.label: Greek alphabet
nodes.description: Greeks repurposed unused Phoenician consonants as vowels, producing the first script that fully encoded both. Made detailed phonetic transcription possible and underwrote later Latin, Cyrillic, and Coptic alphabets.

nodes.label: Aramaic as lingua franca
nodes.description: The administrative tongue first of the Neo-Assyrian and then the Achaemenid Persian empires, spreading from the Nile to the Indus. Modeled how a single language could carry knowledge across political and ethnic boundaries.

nodes.label: Panini's Sanskrit grammar
nodes.description: The Ashtadhyayi specified Sanskrit phonology and morphology with roughly 4000 ordered, generative rules. Anticipated formal grammars by more than two millennia and influenced 20th-century linguistics and computer science.

nodes.label: Qin standardization of Chinese script
nodes.description: Under the first emperor, regional Warring States variants were unified into a single seal script. A founding act of administrative integration that kept literate Chinese culturally continuous across millennia.

nodes.label: Maya hieroglyphic writing
nodes.description: A logosyllabic script encoding the Maya language of Mesoamerica, intricate enough to record ritual, dynastic, and astronomical detail. Independently developed; largely lost after Spanish suppression and rediscovered in the 20th century.

nodes.label: Babylonian sexagesimal mathematics
nodes.description: Old Babylonian scribes used a base-60 place-value system to compute reciprocals, square roots, and Pythagorean triples (Plimpton 322). Foundation for the 60-minute hour and 360-degree circle.

nodes.label: Rhind mathematical papyrus
nodes.description: An Egyptian scribe's compendium of arithmetic, fractions, and geometric problems. Together with the Moscow papyrus, the principal record of pre-Greek mathematical practice.

nodes.label: Thales and deductive geometry
nodes.description: Thales of Miletus is credited with the first attempts to deduce geometric truths from explicit principles rather than rule-of-thumb. Marks the conceptual shift from practical surveying to demonstrative mathematics.

nodes.label: Pythagorean mathematics
nodes.description: The Pythagorean school treated number as the underlying structure of the cosmos. Their work on numerical ratios in music and the right-triangle theorem fused mathematics, philosophy, and religion.

nodes.label: Zeno's paradoxes
nodes.description: Zeno of Elea posed paradoxes of motion and divisibility that probed the coherence of infinite divisibility. Long-resonating provocations that the calculus eventually addressed two millennia later.

nodes.label: Archimedes' mechanics and methods
nodes.description: Archimedes derived areas, volumes, and the law of the lever using exhaustion and balance arguments. His Method, rediscovered in 1906, shows him using infinitesimal-style heuristics two millennia early.

nodes.label: Apollonius on conic sections
nodes.description: A systematic theory of ellipses, parabolas, and hyperbolas as plane sections of a cone. Provided the mathematical objects Kepler later identified with planetary orbits.

nodes.label: Hipparchus and trigonometry
nodes.description: Hipparchus of Nicaea compiled the first known trigonometric tables of chords, used to predict eclipses and locate stars. The empirical core of Greek mathematical astronomy.

nodes.label: Diophantus' Arithmetica
nodes.description: A treatise on solving determinate and indeterminate equations in rational numbers. Its surviving books prefigured symbolic algebra and inspired Fermat, who scribbled his famous marginal note in a copy.

nodes.label: Aryabhata's astronomy and trigonometry
nodes.description: Aryabhata's Aryabhatiya gave a sine table, an excellent value of pi, and a rotating-Earth model. Helped establish the place-value, decimal, sine-based mathematical astronomy that later passed into the Islamic world.

nodes.label: Maya zero and Long Count
nodes.description: Mesoamerican scribes used a positional vigesimal system with an explicit zero glyph to track multi-millennium Long Count dates. An independent invention of zero, contemporary with or earlier than India's.

nodes.label: Daoism and the Daodejing
nodes.description: Attributed to Laozi, the Daodejing articulated a metaphysics of spontaneous, non-coercive process and a politics of restrained governance. Shaped Chinese thought, statecraft, and aesthetics for two millennia.

nodes.label: Confucian ethics
nodes.description: Confucius reframed virtue, ritual, and learning as the foundation of social order. His teachings, gathered in the Analects, became the canonical political-moral framework of East Asian states.

nodes.label: Buddhist analysis of mind
nodes.description: Siddhartha Gautama's framework of impermanence, dependent origination, and the analytic dissection of experience. The Pali canon's later schools produced sustained philosophical work on causation, perception, and selfhood.

nodes.label: Mohist logic and disputation
nodes.description: The Mohist canons, developed by followers of Mozi in Warring States China, contained an explicit theory of names, propositions, and inference. China's most sustained pre-modern formal logic, eclipsed politically when Confucianism became orthodoxy.

nodes.label: Socratic method
nodes.description: Socrates pursued ethical truth by elenchus - cross-examining received beliefs until contradictions surfaced. The model of philosophy as conversation rather than doctrine, transmitted through Plato's dialogues.

nodes.label: Plato's theory of Forms
nodes.description: Plato argued for a realm of timeless, intelligible Forms underlying changeable appearance. Set the agenda - and the foil - for nearly all subsequent Western metaphysics and theology.

nodes.label: Aristotelian natural philosophy
nodes.description: Aristotle's Physics and On the Heavens gave a teleological account of motion, change, and cosmic structure with a stationary Earth and four sublunary elements. The dominant physics in Europe and the Islamic world until the seventeenth century.

nodes.label: Aristotelian biology
nodes.description: Aristotle and his student Theophrastus produced detailed descriptions of hundreds of animal and plant species, with classifications and developmental observations. The first systematic biology.

nodes.label: Theophrastus' botany
nodes.description: Historia Plantarum classified roughly 500 plants by morphology, life cycle, and habitat. Western botany's foundational text, repeatedly recovered through medieval and Renaissance manuscript traditions.

nodes.label: Stoic philosophy
nodes.description: Zeno of Citium and successors built a philosophy of nature, logic, and ethics centered on living according to reason and indifference to externals. Profoundly shaped Roman thought and resurfaces in modern psychology.

nodes.label: Epicurean atomism
nodes.description: Epicurus extended Democritus' atomism into a complete physical and ethical naturalism, transmitted to the modern world through Lucretius' poem. A speculative anticipation of materialist science.

nodes.label: Nyaya logic and epistemology
nodes.description: The Nyaya Sutras codified Indian formal logic, with explicit rules of inference and a systematic theory of valid means of knowledge. India's parallel to the Aristotelian-Stoic tradition.

nodes.label: Chinese legalism (Han Feizi)
nodes.description: Han Fei synthesized realist political theory: rule by codified, impersonal law backed by clear sanctions. Provided the operating philosophy of the Qin unification of China.

nodes.label: Augustine's theology of inwardness
nodes.description: Confessions and City of God fused Platonism with Christianity and pioneered the introspective first-person mode that runs from Descartes to phenomenology.

nodes.label: Babylonian observational astronomy
nodes.description: Centuries of dated planetary and lunar positions recorded on cuneiform tablets, used to predict celestial phenomena by arithmetic schemes. The earliest sustained quantitative science.

nodes.label: Aristarchus' heliocentric proposal
nodes.description: Aristarchus of Samos proposed that the Earth orbits a central Sun and estimated the relative sizes of Sun, Moon, and Earth. A speculative heliocentrism rejected for empirical and philosophical reasons until Copernicus.

nodes.label: Eratosthenes measures the Earth
nodes.description: Comparing solar shadow lengths at Syene and Alexandria, Eratosthenes computed the circumference of the Earth to within a few percent. The first quantitative result in physical geography.

nodes.label: Strabo's Geography
nodes.description: A 17-book synthesis of physical, political, and cultural geography of the inhabited world known to Rome. Modeled how diverse observations could be assembled into a coherent earth-science narrative.

nodes.label: Ptolemy's Almagest
nodes.description: Ptolemy's geocentric mathematical astronomy, with deferents, epicycles, and equants, predicted celestial positions accurately enough to anchor calendrical and astrological practice for fourteen centuries.

nodes.label: Zhang Heng's seismoscope
nodes.description: A bronze vessel with eight dragon-mouths whose pendulum mechanism dropped a ball in the direction of a distant earthquake. The earliest known instrument for detecting seismic events.

nodes.label: Han Chinese supernova record
nodes.description: Chinese court astronomers recorded a 'guest star' visible for months in 185 CE - now identified with the supernova remnant RCW 86. One of the earliest dated astronomical events used by modern science.

nodes.label: Imhotep and Egyptian medicine
nodes.description: Vizier and architect of the Step Pyramid, later deified as a healer. Anchors a long Egyptian tradition of pharmacopeia, surgical practice, and sacred medicine recorded in later papyri.

nodes.label: Edwin Smith surgical papyrus
nodes.description: An Egyptian treatise of 48 trauma cases, each with diagnosis, prognosis, and treatment. Strikingly empirical and largely free of magical content - the oldest surviving systematic medical text.

nodes.label: Sushruta Samhita
nodes.description: An Indian compendium describing more than 300 surgical procedures, including cataract extraction and rhinoplasty, plus a surgical pedagogy on dissection of cadavers. A founding text of operative medicine.

nodes.label: Huangdi Neijing (Yellow Emperor's Inner Canon)
nodes.description: The foundational text of Chinese medicine, organized as dialogues on physiology, diagnosis, qi, meridians, acupuncture, and pharmacology. Frame for two millennia of East Asian medical practice.

nodes.label: Charaka Samhita
nodes.description: An Indian compendium codifying internal medicine, diagnosis, ethics, and herbal pharmacology in the Ayurvedic tradition. Together with the Sushruta Samhita the canonical medical literature of South Asia.

nodes.label: Dioscorides' De Materia Medica
nodes.description: A Greek physician's catalogue of about 600 plants and 1000 drugs with sources, preparation, and indications. The standard pharmacological reference in Europe and the Islamic world for fifteen centuries.

nodes.label: Galenic anatomy and physiology
nodes.description: Galen's experimental work on nerves, the heart, and humoral physiology synthesized Greco-Roman medicine. His authority went unchallenged in Europe and the Islamic world until Vesalius and Harvey.

nodes.label: Pliny's Natural History
nodes.description: Pliny the Elder's 37-book encyclopedia compiled cosmology, geography, anthropology, zoology, botany, and mineralogy from hundreds of earlier sources. The model of an integrated natural history that ran from antiquity to Buffon.

nodes.label: Great Wall of China (Qin)
nodes.description: Qin unification connected and extended earlier ramparts into a continental defensive system. A sustained logistics, surveying, and labor-management feat across mountainous terrain.

nodes.label: Roman aqueducts and concrete
nodes.description: Aqueducts moving fresh water tens of kilometers, paired with hydraulic concrete (opus caementicium) that set underwater. Civil engineering at a scale not matched in Europe for 1500 years.

nodes.label: Archimedes' screw and mechanics
nodes.description: Archimedes designed water-lifting screws, compound pulleys, and siege engines while developing the theoretical mechanics that explained them. An early fusion of engineering practice and mathematical science.

nodes.label: Lodestone compass in China
nodes.description: Han-era diviners used naturally magnetized 'south-pointing' lodestone spoons; centuries later, magnetized iron needles became navigational compasses. The first instrument coupling Earth's magnetic field to human orientation.

nodes.label: Sun Tzu's Art of War
nodes.description: A treatise on strategy framing conflict as deception, terrain, and morale. Among the earliest works to abstract competition between organized actors into general principles.

nodes.label: Chanakya's Arthashastra
nodes.description: An Indian manual of statecraft, economics, taxation, and intelligence services for the Mauryan empire. A remarkably hard-headed pre-modern work of political economy.

nodes.label: Thucydides' Peloponnesian War
nodes.description: A history written to extract general lessons about power, fear, and self-interest from contemporary events. Founded the analytic, source-critical mode of historiography.

nodes.label: Herodotus' Histories
nodes.description: A sprawling inquiry (historia) into the customs, geographies, and conflicts of the peoples of the eastern Mediterranean. The first sustained ethnography in the Western tradition.

nodes.label: Jabir's experimental alchemy
nodes.description: Jabir ibn Hayyan formalized procedures of distillation, sublimation, and crystallization in Abbasid Baghdad. Established laboratory practice and a chemical nomenclature that the Latin world inherited as alchemy.

nodes.label: House of Wisdom translation movement
nodes.description: Caliphal patronage in Abbasid Baghdad rendered Greek, Persian, Syriac, and Sanskrit science into Arabic. A vast inheritance later retransmitted to Europe through Andalusia and Sicily.

nodes.label: Gunpowder in Tang and Song China
nodes.description: Daoist alchemists combined saltpeter, sulfur, and charcoal into a deflagrating mixture, soon weaponized into rockets, bombs, and the earliest firearms. Reshaped war, mining, and global power balances after diffusing west.

nodes.label: Al-Razi's clinical medicine
nodes.description: Rhazes ran a Baghdad hospital, produced the first clear differential diagnosis of smallpox and measles, and authored the encyclopedic Kitab al-Hawi. A model of disciplined clinical observation.

nodes.label: Al-Biruni's geodesy and mineralogy
nodes.description: Al-Biruni measured the Earth's radius with a single mountain observation, catalogued specific gravities of minerals, and described the Indus valley as a former seabed. A premodern earth-scientist of unusual range.

nodes.label: Avicenna's Canon of Medicine
nodes.description: Ibn Sina's al-Qanun synthesized Greco-Roman, Indian, and Arabic medicine into a single coherent system. The standard medical textbook from Bukhara to Bologna for half a millennium.

nodes.label: Avicennan metaphysics
nodes.description: Avicenna distinguished essence from existence, articulated a proof of a necessary being, and shaped both later Islamic kalam and Latin scholastic theology - read attentively by Aquinas.

nodes.label: Crab Nebula supernova (SN 1054)
nodes.description: Chinese and Arab astronomers recorded a 'guest star' bright enough to see by day for weeks - the explosion now identified with the Crab Nebula. Modern astrophysics relies on these dated records to time pulsar physics.

nodes.label: Bi Sheng's movable type
nodes.description: A Northern Song artisan cast individual ceramic characters that could be set, inked, and reused. The principle later carried through Korean metal type and Gutenberg's press.

nodes.label: Su Song's astronomical clock tower
nodes.description: A 12-meter water-driven clock tower with an escapement, a celestial globe, and an armillary sphere. The most complex mechanical instrument in the world for its century.

nodes.label: Omar Khayyam's algebra and calendar
nodes.description: Khayyam classified cubic equations geometrically and led the calendar reform that produced the Jalali calendar - more accurate than the later Gregorian by a wide margin.

nodes.label: Mariner's compass (Song China)
nodes.description: Shen Kuo's 1088 description of magnetic declination and floating-needle compasses on Chinese ships. Adopted by Arab and European mariners within two centuries, enabling deep-ocean navigation.

nodes.label: Fibonacci's Liber Abaci
nodes.description: Leonardo of Pisa imported Hindu-Arabic numerals into Latin Europe with practical merchant arithmetic. Catalyzed the long, slow displacement of Roman numerals across European bookkeeping.

nodes.label: Ibn al-Nafis discovers pulmonary circulation
nodes.description: The Cairo physician traced blood from the right heart through the lungs to the left heart, contradicting Galen on a key anatomical point three centuries before Servetus and Harvey.

nodes.label: Aquinas' Summa Theologica
nodes.description: Aquinas synthesized Aristotelian natural philosophy with Christian theology and articulated five proofs for God's existence. The intellectual backbone of medieval scholasticism.

nodes.label: Oresme's geometry of motion
nodes.description: Nicole Oresme graphed velocity against time and proved the mean-speed theorem for uniformly accelerated motion. A geometric anticipation of kinematics that Galileo would re-derive.

nodes.label: Ibn Khaldun's Muqaddimah
nodes.description: Ibn Khaldun analyzed the rise and fall of polities through group solidarity (asabiyyah), urban-rural dynamics, and labor specialization. A founding work of sociology, historiography, and economics.

nodes.label: Madhava and the Kerala school of analysis
nodes.description: Madhava of Sangamagrama derived power-series expansions for sine, cosine, and the inverse tangent - including the Madhava-Leibniz series for pi - two centuries before European calculus.

nodes.label: Hangul phonetic alphabet (Korea)
nodes.description: King Sejong the Great commissioned a featural alphabet whose letter shapes encode articulatory phonetics. A rare case of deliberate, theoretically grounded script design that became broadly literate.

nodes.label: Machiavelli's Prince
nodes.description: Florentine diplomat's blunt analysis of how power is acquired and kept, divorcing political effectiveness from inherited moral categories. Helped found modern political science.

nodes.label: Paracelsus' iatrochemistry
nodes.description: Paracelsus replaced humoral medicine with chemical pharmacology, advocating mineral remedies and dose-dependent toxicity. A confrontational forerunner of pharmacology.

nodes.label: Vesalius' De humani corporis fabrica
nodes.description: Andreas Vesalius dissected human cadavers in Padua and published meticulously illustrated anatomical books that overturned dozens of Galenic errors. The empirical refoundation of human anatomy.

nodes.label: Ambroise Pare's battlefield surgery
nodes.description: Pare replaced cauterization with vessel ligation and developed humane wound dressings during French campaigns. Reformed surgery from a barber's trade toward a learned profession.

nodes.label: Tycho Brahe's planetary observations
nodes.description: Tycho built Uraniborg and amassed planetary positions with naked-eye precision unmatched until the telescope. His data was the empirical bedrock from which Kepler extracted elliptical orbits.

nodes.label: Bodin on sovereignty
nodes.description: Jean Bodin's Six Books of the Commonwealth defined sovereignty as supreme, perpetual, and indivisible state power. Bridged late medieval and modern political theory.

nodes.label: Galileo's telescopic astronomy
nodes.description: Galileo turned an improved telescope skyward and reported lunar mountains, Jovian moons, and phases of Venus. Evidence that the heavens were structured matter, not perfect spheres - and that Aristotelian cosmology was wrong.

nodes.label: Kepler's laws of planetary motion
nodes.description: Kepler distilled Tycho's data into three laws: elliptical orbits, equal areas in equal times, and the period-distance relation. Replaced two millennia of circular astronomy and seeded Newtonian gravity.

nodes.label: Napier's logarithms
nodes.description: John Napier published tables that turned multiplication into addition by exponents. With Briggs' base-10 tables they collapsed the labor of astronomical and navigational computation by an order of magnitude.

nodes.label: Grotius on the law of war and peace
nodes.description: Hugo Grotius derived a natural-law theory of relations between states from premises he claimed would hold even if there were no God. Founded modern international law.

nodes.label: Harvey describes blood circulation
nodes.description: William Harvey's De Motu Cordis demonstrated by quantitative argument and dissection that blood circulates in a closed loop driven by the heart. The decisive break with Galenic physiology.

nodes.label: Descartes' analytic geometry
nodes.description: La Geometrie fused algebra and Euclidean geometry by treating curves as equations in coordinates. Gave physics a language in which space could be calculated upon.

nodes.label: Cartesian dualism (Meditations)
nodes.description: Descartes' radical doubt grounded knowledge in the thinking subject and split reality into thinking substance and extended substance. The starting move of modern Western philosophy.

nodes.label: Pascal's mechanical calculator
nodes.description: The Pascaline performed addition and subtraction via gear trains. Together with Leibniz's later stepped-drum calculator, the first practical mechanical computation.

nodes.label: Cartesian mechanism (Principia Philosophiae)
nodes.description: Descartes proposed that physics is the mechanics of contact between extended bodies in a plenum, with vortices carrying planets. A mechanistic worldview that Newtonian forces eventually displaced.

nodes.label: Hobbes' Leviathan
nodes.description: Hobbes derived the necessity of an absolute sovereign from a thought experiment about prepolitical individuals. The first systematic social-contract argument in Western thought.

nodes.label: Pascal-Fermat correspondence on probability
nodes.description: Letters analyzing gambling problems gave mathematical form to expectation and probability. The seed of a discipline that runs to insurance, statistical mechanics, and Bayesian inference.

nodes.label: Huygens' pendulum clock
nodes.description: Christiaan Huygens built a clock regulated by an isochronous pendulum, two orders of magnitude more accurate than predecessors. Enabled astronomical observation, longitude work, and quantitative experimental physics.

nodes.label: Boyle's Sceptical Chymist
nodes.description: Robert Boyle attacked the Aristotelian four elements and argued for testing chemical claims experimentally with corpuscular hypotheses. Set the methodological tone for the chemistry that Lavoisier completed.

nodes.label: Boyle's gas law
nodes.description: Boyle and Hooke's experiments showed that gas pressure varies inversely with volume at constant temperature. The first quantitative law of a gas - and an early piece of what became thermodynamics.

nodes.label: Hooke's Micrographia
nodes.description: Robert Hooke's illustrated micrographs of plants, insects, and minerals introduced the term 'cell' for cork pores. The first widely circulated demonstration of the microscope as a scientific tool.

nodes.label: Malpighi's microscopic anatomy
nodes.description: Marcello Malpighi's microscope work resolved capillaries linking arteries to veins, taste buds, and pulmonary alveoli. Closed Harvey's circulation theory by visualizing its missing vessels.

nodes.label: Steno's principles of stratigraphy
nodes.description: Nicolas Steno articulated superposition, original horizontality, and lateral continuity, and recognized fossils as organic remains. The conceptual foundation of historical geology.

nodes.label: Grew's plant anatomy
nodes.description: Nehemiah Grew described plant tissues, vascular bundles, and pollen with microscopic precision. Together with Malpighi, founded scientific botany.

nodes.label: Leeuwenhoek observes microorganisms
nodes.description: Antonie van Leeuwenhoek's hand-ground single-lens microscopes resolved bacteria, protozoa, and spermatozoa - a hidden living world. Reported to the Royal Society and astonishing his contemporaries.

nodes.label: Spinoza's Ethics
nodes.description: Spinoza derived a monist pantheistic philosophy in geometrical form, identifying God with the single infinite substance of which mind and matter are attributes. A landmark of rationalist metaphysics.

nodes.label: Sydenham's clinical observation
nodes.description: Thomas Sydenham championed bedside observation, careful descriptive case histories, and disease as a species - reasserting the Hippocratic posture inside emerging modern medicine.

nodes.label: Locke's Essay Concerning Human Understanding
nodes.description: Locke argued the mind starts as tabula rasa and acquires ideas through sensation and reflection. Founded modern empiricist epistemology and informed liberal political thought.

nodes.label: Locke's Two Treatises of Government
nodes.description: Locke grounded government in consent and natural rights to life, liberty, and property. Direct intellectual ancestor of American and French revolutionary constitutionalism.

nodes.label: Huygens' wave theory of light
nodes.description: Treatise on Light proposed that light propagates as wavefronts whose envelopes generate refraction and reflection. The competing alternative to Newton's corpuscular theory, vindicated a century later by Young.

nodes.label: Savery's atmospheric steam pump
nodes.description: Thomas Savery patented a steam-driven mine-drainage pump using vacuum condensation. Newcomen improved it in 1712 - the first practical use of steam power on an industrial scale.

nodes.label: Leibniz's binary arithmetic
nodes.description: Leibniz published an explicit account of base-2 arithmetic, inspired in part by the Yijing. The number system that, two and a half centuries later, would become the native language of digital circuits.

nodes.label: Linnaean binomial nomenclature
nodes.description: Linnaeus' Systema Naturae imposed a hierarchical, two-name classification on living things. A practical scaffolding still used to organize biodiversity.

nodes.label: Euler's analysis and notation
nodes.description: Euler unified analysis around the function concept and introduced much of modern notation (e, i, pi, sigma, f(x)). Wrote enough material to fill 70 volumes - the most prolific mathematician of his century.

nodes.label: Buffon's Histoire Naturelle
nodes.description: Buffon's 36-volume natural history pushed Earth's age toward 75,000 years, classified mammals comparatively, and entertained ideas of common descent. Read across Enlightenment Europe.

nodes.label: Watt's separate condenser
nodes.description: James Watt's separate-condenser steam engine roughly tripled efficiency and made steam power competitive in factories, mines, transport, and metallurgy. The defining engine of the Industrial Revolution.

nodes.label: Priestley isolates oxygen
nodes.description: Joseph Priestley liberated 'dephlogisticated air' from mercuric oxide. Lavoisier soon identified it as oxygen and built combustion chemistry around it.

nodes.label: Kant's Critique of Pure Reason
nodes.description: Kant argued that space, time, and causality are not features of the world per se but conditions of possible experience. Reframed the empiricism-rationalism debate and set the agenda for two centuries of philosophy.

nodes.label: Hutton's Theory of the Earth
nodes.description: James Hutton argued that the Earth's surface is shaped by ongoing slow processes operating over unimaginable spans - 'no vestige of a beginning, no prospect of an end.' Deep time enters geology.

nodes.label: Lagrange's analytical mechanics
nodes.description: Mecanique analytique recast Newtonian mechanics in terms of generalized coordinates and a single variational principle. The form physics still uses to derive equations of motion in arbitrary settings.

nodes.label: Jenner's smallpox vaccination
nodes.description: Edward Jenner showed that cowpox inoculation protected against smallpox. The first scientific vaccine and the proof of concept that disease could be deliberately prevented - the eventual model for eradication.

nodes.label: Malthus' Essay on Population
nodes.description: Malthus argued that population grows geometrically while food grows arithmetically. A pessimistic counter to Enlightenment progressivism and a key influence on both Darwin and Wallace.

nodes.label: Volta's electrochemical pile
nodes.description: Alessandro Volta stacked zinc-copper-electrolyte cells to produce continuous electric current. Made electrochemistry, electromagnetism, and electrolysis experimentally tractable.

nodes.label: Young's double-slit experiment
nodes.description: Thomas Young demonstrated interference fringes from two coherent light sources, providing decisive evidence for the wave nature of light. Returns more than a century later as a centerpiece of quantum mechanics.

nodes.label: Gauss' Disquisitiones Arithmeticae
nodes.description: Gauss synthesized modular arithmetic, quadratic reciprocity, and the construction of regular polygons. Founded modern number theory and announced its 'queen of mathematics' status.

nodes.label: Jacquard punched-card loom
nodes.description: A loom whose weaving pattern was set by a chain of punched cards. The earliest production-scale programmable machine - and a direct conceptual ancestor of Babbage and IBM.

nodes.label: Hegel's Phenomenology of Spirit
nodes.description: Hegel narrated the historical self-development of consciousness through dialectical stages. Profoundly shaped 19th-century philosophy, theology, and the Marxist tradition.

nodes.label: Dalton's atomic theory
nodes.description: John Dalton posited that each element is a distinct kind of atom with a characteristic weight, and compounds form by simple integer ratios. A quantitative atomism that gave Lavoisier's chemistry a microphysical interpretation.

nodes.label: Avogadro's molecular hypothesis
nodes.description: Amedeo Avogadro proposed that equal volumes of gases at the same temperature and pressure contain equal numbers of molecules. Ignored for half a century, then central to the determination of atomic weights.

nodes.label: Cuvier founds paleontology
nodes.description: Georges Cuvier used comparative anatomy to reconstruct extinct vertebrates and demonstrated that whole faunas had vanished. Forced naturalists to accept that the deep past held creatures unlike anything living.

nodes.label: Laplace's analytic theory of probability
nodes.description: Laplace systematized probability theory, generating functions, and central-limit results, and laid out a deterministic 'demon' for whom nothing is uncertain. Founded mathematical statistics.

nodes.label: Ricardo's political economy
nodes.description: David Ricardo derived comparative advantage, the labor theory of value, and rent theory in deductive form. The discipline's first attempt at a rigorous analytical economics.

nodes.label: Fourier's theory of heat and series
nodes.description: Joseph Fourier's Analytical Theory of Heat derived diffusion equations and represented arbitrary functions as sums of sines and cosines. Foundational for partial differential equations, signal processing, and quantum mechanics.

nodes.label: Babbage's difference and analytical engines
nodes.description: Charles Babbage designed automatic calculation machines - the analytical engine of 1837 with stored program, conditional branching, and memory. A century-early blueprint of the general-purpose computer.

nodes.label: Braille tactile script
nodes.description: Louis Braille adapted a military night-writing scheme into a six-dot tactile alphabet readable by the blind. The first widely adopted assistive writing system.

nodes.label: Carnot's cycle and the second law
nodes.description: Sadi Carnot identified a theoretical limit on heat-engine efficiency dependent only on temperatures, not on working substance. Seed of the second law of thermodynamics and the concept of entropy.

nodes.label: Lyell's Principles of Geology
nodes.description: Charles Lyell argued for uniformitarianism - that present-day processes acting over immense times explain past landscapes. The book Darwin took aboard the Beagle.

nodes.label: Faraday's electromagnetic induction
nodes.description: Michael Faraday discovered that a changing magnetic flux induces an electromotive force. Together with the inverse Oersted effect, the principles behind motors, generators, and Maxwell's later synthesis.

nodes.label: Galois theory of equations
nodes.description: Evariste Galois showed that the solvability of polynomial equations by radicals depends on the symmetry group of their roots. Introduced groups as a tool and opened modern abstract algebra.

nodes.label: Comte's positivism and sociology
nodes.description: Auguste Comte proposed a hierarchy of sciences crowned by 'sociology' and a methodology limited to observable regularities. Named the field that Durkheim and Weber would build.

nodes.label: Non-Euclidean geometry
nodes.description: Lobachevsky, Bolyai, and (privately) Gauss developed consistent geometries denying Euclid's parallel postulate. The proof that geometry is not unique and that experience must decide which one describes physical space.

nodes.label: Cell theory
nodes.description: Schleiden and Schwann argued that all living tissues are composed of cells and that cells are the fundamental units of life. Together with Virchow's 'omnis cellula e cellula' (1855), the cellular paradigm of biology.

nodes.label: Agassiz and ice ages
nodes.description: Louis Agassiz showed by glacial erratics, striations, and moraines that much of Europe had once been ice-covered. Established that climate, and the Earth's surface, have radically changed.

nodes.label: Photography (daguerreotype)
nodes.description: Daguerre's silver-iodide process fixed an image from a camera obscura. Within decades photography transformed art, journalism, criminology, astronomy, and biological documentation.

nodes.label: Ada Lovelace on Babbage's analytical engine
nodes.description: Lovelace's notes on the analytical engine include the first published algorithm intended for machine execution and a striking discussion of its general symbolic capabilities. Arguably the first piece of computer science writing.

nodes.label: Electric telegraph
nodes.description: Samuel Morse's working telegraph line and Morse code dispatch enabled near-instantaneous long-distance text. The first technology to decouple the speed of information from the speed of transport.

nodes.label: Humboldt's Kosmos
nodes.description: Alexander von Humboldt synthesized observations from his American and Eurasian expeditions into an integrated picture of physical geography, climate, and biogeography. A prototype of earth-system science.

nodes.label: Ether anesthesia
nodes.description: William Morton's public demonstration of ether anesthesia at Massachusetts General Hospital. Surgery became survivable as a routine practice rather than a brutal last resort.

nodes.label: Helmholtz and conservation of energy
nodes.description: Hermann von Helmholtz argued from first principles that energy in its mechanical, thermal, chemical, and electromagnetic forms is conserved. Crystallized the first law of thermodynamics.

nodes.label: Semmelweis and antisepsis
nodes.description: Ignaz Semmelweis showed that hand washing with chlorinated lime collapsed maternal mortality from puerperal fever. Pre-germ-theory evidence-based prophylaxis, tragically rejected in his lifetime.

nodes.label: Marx's Capital
nodes.description: Marx analyzed capitalist production via labor theory of value, surplus extraction, and tendencies of accumulation. Reframed economics as a critical, historical discipline and seeded the 20th-century left.

nodes.label: Boolean algebra
nodes.description: George Boole's Laws of Thought represented logical propositions as algebra over the values 0 and 1. The mathematical structure that 80 years later would describe digital circuits.

nodes.label: Snow's Broad Street cholera map
nodes.description: John Snow plotted cholera deaths around a Soho water pump and inferred a waterborne agent decades before Vibrio cholerae was isolated. Founded the spatial-statistical method of epidemiology.

nodes.label: Riemannian geometry
nodes.description: Bernhard Riemann's habilitation lecture generalized geometry to arbitrary smooth manifolds with a metric. The mathematical setting Einstein needed for general relativity sixty years later.

nodes.label: Bessemer process for steel
nodes.description: Blowing air through molten pig iron oxidized impurities and produced large quantities of cheap structural steel. The defining material of late-19th-century railways, ships, and skyscrapers.

nodes.label: Nightingale's health statistics
nodes.description: Florence Nightingale used statistical graphics (the rose diagram) to show that most Crimean War deaths were from preventable disease, not combat. Helped found evidence-based public health and modern nursing.

nodes.label: Mill's On Liberty
nodes.description: John Stuart Mill defended liberty of thought, expression, and self-regarding action against state and majority coercion. The classical statement of liberal political philosophy.

nodes.label: Kekule's benzene ring
nodes.description: August Kekule proposed a hexagonal cyclic structure for benzene, accounting for its peculiar chemistry. Crucial for the rise of structural organic chemistry and industrial dyestuffs.

nodes.label: Mendel's laws of inheritance
nodes.description: Gregor Mendel's pea-plant experiments demonstrated discrete factors of inheritance combining in predictable ratios. Ignored for 34 years, then central to the modern synthesis.

nodes.label: Clausius and entropy
nodes.description: Rudolf Clausius gave the second law of thermodynamics its modern form and named the new state quantity 'entropy.' Set the stage for statistical interpretation in the 1870s.

nodes.label: The typewriter
nodes.description: Christopher Sholes' QWERTY keyboard typewriter, commercialized by Remington in 1874. Reshaped office work, journalism, and women's employment, and seeded the keyboards still attached to every digital device.

nodes.label: Cantor's set theory
nodes.description: Georg Cantor proved that the real numbers cannot be put in one-to-one correspondence with the integers and developed a graded hierarchy of infinities. Founded set theory and shocked his contemporaries.

nodes.label: Otto's four-stroke engine
nodes.description: Nikolaus Otto's compressed-charge four-stroke cycle gave a working internal-combustion engine. Within two decades it powered automobiles, motorcycles, and small machinery.

nodes.label: The telephone
nodes.description: Alexander Graham Bell's transmission of articulated speech over wires. Within fifty years the telephone created a continent-spanning real-time voice network and a new economic giant in AT&T.

nodes.label: Edison's phonograph
nodes.description: The first device to record and reproduce sound. Made speech and music storable and tradable, launching modern audio culture.

nodes.label: Edison's electric light and grid
nodes.description: A practical incandescent bulb, plus the central station system to power one. Made cities, factories, and homes electrically illuminated and seeded universal electrification.

nodes.label: Frege's predicate logic
nodes.description: Gottlob Frege's Begriffsschrift introduced quantifiers and a fully formal language for mathematics. The biggest advance in logic since Aristotle and the substrate for Russell, Goedel, and computing.

nodes.label: Pasteur's attenuated vaccines
nodes.description: Louis Pasteur developed attenuated vaccines for anthrax (1881) and rabies (1885), inaugurating modern vaccinology. Built directly on his germ-theory experiments.

nodes.label: Michelson-Morley experiment
nodes.description: An interferometer designed to detect the Earth's motion through the luminiferous aether saw nothing. The empirical embarrassment that special relativity resolved.

nodes.label: Boasian cultural anthropology
nodes.description: Franz Boas attacked racial typologies and unilinear cultural evolution, insisting on fieldwork, language learning, and historical particularism. Reshaped anthropology away from 19th-century evolutionism.

nodes.label: Tesla's AC induction motor
nodes.description: Nikola Tesla's polyphase alternating-current motor enabled the long-distance transmission of electric power. The 'AC vs. DC' war was decided in favor of efficient grid-scale electricity.

nodes.label: Cajal's neuron doctrine
nodes.description: Santiago Ramon y Cajal used Golgi's silver stain to show that the nervous system is built from discrete, polarized neurons that communicate across gaps. The cellular foundation of neuroscience.

nodes.label: James' Principles of Psychology
nodes.description: William James synthesized physiological, philosophical, and introspective psychology into a single discipline organized around the stream of consciousness, habit, and emotion.

nodes.label: Durkheim's Division of Labor in Society
nodes.description: Emile Durkheim treated social facts as objects, analyzed organic versus mechanical solidarity, and made sociology an empirical discipline in the universities. Suicide (1897) followed with quantitative case studies.

nodes.label: Roentgen discovers X-rays
nodes.description: Wilhelm Roentgen's accidental discovery of penetrating radiation from a cathode-ray tube. Within months X-ray imaging was in clinical use; within a decade it opened crystallography and quantum physics.

nodes.label: Becquerel discovers radioactivity
nodes.description: Henri Becquerel's accidental finding that uranium salts spontaneously emit penetrating rays. Marie and Pierre Curie soon isolated polonium and radium - and a window into the atomic nucleus.

nodes.label: Arrhenius and CO2 climate sensitivity
nodes.description: Svante Arrhenius computed the warming expected from doubling atmospheric CO2 - the first quantitative climate-sensitivity estimate. Sat largely dormant until the 1950s recovered it.

nodes.label: Pavlov's classical conditioning
nodes.description: Ivan Pavlov's controlled experiments showed that involuntary responses can be conditioned to neutral stimuli. The behavioral foundation that Watson and Skinner later extended.

nodes.label: Aspirin (acetylsalicylic acid)
nodes.description: Felix Hoffmann at Bayer synthesized a stable, palatable form of salicylic acid. The first blockbuster modern drug and the cornerstone of industrial pharmacology.

nodes.label: Discovery of polonium and radium
nodes.description: Marie and Pierre Curie isolated new radioactive elements from pitchblende, founding radiochemistry. Their work transformed both physics and medicine.

nodes.label: Freud's Interpretation of Dreams
nodes.description: Sigmund Freud's argument that dreams reveal unconscious wishes inaugurated psychoanalysis. Empirically contested but culturally pervasive, shaping psychology, literature, and self-understanding throughout the 20th century.

nodes.label: Planck's quantum hypothesis
nodes.description: Max Planck modeled blackbody radiation by quantizing the energy of oscillators. A reluctant act of theoretical desperation that started the quantum revolution.

nodes.label: Powered heavier-than-air flight
nodes.description: The Wright brothers' Flyer at Kitty Hawk demonstrated controllable, powered, sustained flight. Within sixty years humans were in orbit.

nodes.label: Special relativity
nodes.description: Einstein's annus mirabilis paper redefined space, time, and mass-energy through the constancy of the speed of light. Resolved the Michelson-Morley puzzle and rewired classical mechanics at high speed.

nodes.label: Einstein's photoelectric effect
nodes.description: Einstein extended Planck's quantum hypothesis to light itself, proposing photons. Earned him the Nobel Prize and forced quantum theory on the wider physics community.

nodes.label: Weber's Protestant Ethic
nodes.description: Max Weber argued that ascetic Protestantism inadvertently helped birth modern capitalism. Together with Economy and Society (1921), defined sociology as a comparative interpretive discipline.

nodes.label: Bakelite, the first synthetic plastic
nodes.description: Leo Baekeland's phenol-formaldehyde resin was the first wholly synthetic, mass-produced polymer. Started the materials revolution that produced 20th-century consumer goods.

nodes.label: Haber-Bosch nitrogen fixation
nodes.description: Fritz Haber and Carl Bosch synthesized ammonia from atmospheric nitrogen at industrial scale. Doubled the human food supply and underwrote modern agriculture and explosives.

nodes.label: Principia Mathematica (Russell-Whitehead)
nodes.description: An attempt to derive all of mathematics from a small set of logical axioms. Both ambitious and partial - Goedel later showed the program could not be completed - but a defining text of mathematical logic.

nodes.label: Rutherford's nuclear atom
nodes.description: Alpha-particle scattering off gold foil revealed the atom's tiny dense nucleus. Killed the plum-pudding model and pointed toward nuclear physics.

nodes.label: Wegener proposes continental drift
nodes.description: Alfred Wegener noted matching coastlines and fossils across the Atlantic and proposed that continents move. Rejected for decades for lack of a mechanism, then vindicated by plate tectonics.

nodes.label: Bohr's atomic model
nodes.description: Niels Bohr quantized electron orbits to explain hydrogen's spectrum. The first quantum picture of the atom and an essential bridge to wave mechanics.

nodes.label: Husserl's phenomenology
nodes.description: Edmund Husserl's Ideas grounded philosophy in the structures of conscious experience. Founded a tradition that runs through Heidegger, Sartre, Merleau-Ponty, and continues today.

nodes.label: Ford's moving assembly line
nodes.description: Henry Ford's Highland Park plant brought the Model T's assembly time from 12 hours to 90 minutes by moving the work past stationary workers. Defined 20th-century mass production.

nodes.label: Morgan's chromosomal theory of inheritance
nodes.description: Thomas Hunt Morgan's fruit-fly experiments tied Mendelian factors to physical positions on chromosomes. Made genetics a quantitative, mechanistic science.

nodes.label: Saussure's structural linguistics
nodes.description: Saussure's posthumous Course in General Linguistics framed language as a system of differential signs. Founded structural linguistics and inspired structuralism across the social sciences.

nodes.label: Keynes on the consequences of the peace
nodes.description: John Maynard Keynes' analysis of the Treaty of Versailles linked macroeconomics to international politics with unusual force. Set the stage for his General Theory and 20th-century macroeconomics.

nodes.label: Hubble's expanding universe
nodes.description: Edwin Hubble showed that distant galaxies recede faster the farther they are. Demonstrated cosmic expansion - the empirical anchor of Big Bang cosmology.

nodes.label: Milankovitch orbital climate cycles
nodes.description: Milutin Milankovitch computed how slow changes in Earth's orbit, tilt, and precession redistribute solar input. Decades later confirmed as the pacemaker of Pleistocene ice ages.

nodes.label: Electronic television
nodes.description: Philo Farnsworth's all-electronic image-dissector camera demonstrated a viable scanned television system. Within decades it was the dominant cultural medium worldwide.

nodes.label: Lemaitre's primeval atom (Big Bang)
nodes.description: Georges Lemaitre derived an expanding-universe solution to general relativity and proposed an explosive primeval origin. Hubble's observations soon supplied empirical traction.

nodes.label: Heidegger's Being and Time
nodes.description: Heidegger reframed metaphysics around the concrete temporal structure of human existence (Dasein). Hugely influential, philosophically and politically controversial.

nodes.label: Dirac equation and antimatter
nodes.description: Paul Dirac unified quantum mechanics with special relativity for the electron and predicted antimatter. The conceptual gateway to quantum field theory.

nodes.label: Fleming discovers penicillin
nodes.description: Alexander Fleming noticed that a Penicillium mold killed bacteria on a contaminated plate. Florey and Chain industrialized it into the antibiotic that transformed 20th-century medicine.

nodes.label: Insulin therapy for diabetes
nodes.description: Banting, Best, and Macleod isolated pancreatic insulin and showed it controlled blood sugar. Type 1 diabetes turned from a death sentence to a manageable chronic disease.

nodes.label: Chandrasekhar limit
nodes.description: Subrahmanyan Chandrasekhar showed white dwarfs above 1.4 solar masses must collapse further. Opened the modern theory of stellar end states - neutron stars and black holes.

nodes.label: Goedel's incompleteness theorems
nodes.description: Kurt Goedel proved that any consistent axiomatic system rich enough for arithmetic contains true statements it cannot prove. Killed Hilbert's program and reframed the limits of formal reasoning.

nodes.label: Zwicky proposes dark matter
nodes.description: Fritz Zwicky observed that galaxies in the Coma Cluster moved too fast to be bound by visible matter alone and posited unseen 'dunkle Materie.' Now a central, still unresolved problem of cosmology.

nodes.label: Popper's falsifiability criterion
nodes.description: Karl Popper proposed falsifiability as the demarcation between science and pseudoscience. Hugely influential on working scientists' self-understanding.

nodes.label: Keynes' General Theory
nodes.description: Keynes argued that aggregate demand could persistently fall short of full-employment output, justifying countercyclical fiscal policy. Founded macroeconomics as a distinct discipline.

nodes.label: Piaget's developmental psychology
nodes.description: Jean Piaget identified successive stages of cognitive development through close observation of children's reasoning. Defined developmental psychology and shaped 20th-century education.

nodes.label: Xerographic copying
nodes.description: Chester Carlson demonstrated electrostatic dry copying. The Xerox 914 (1959) democratized document reproduction and reshaped offices, journalism, and bureaucracy.

nodes.label: Pauling's nature of the chemical bond
nodes.description: Linus Pauling fused quantum mechanics with chemical practice to explain bonding, electronegativity, and resonance. Reset the language of structural chemistry and biochemistry.

nodes.label: Jet engine
nodes.description: Frank Whittle and Hans von Ohain independently demonstrated turbojet flight. Jet propulsion reshaped both warfare and the global travel network within two decades.

nodes.label: Modern evolutionary synthesis
nodes.description: Dobzhansky, Mayr, Simpson, and others fused Darwinian selection with Mendelian genetics and population mathematics. The intellectual armature of 20th-century biology.

nodes.label: Self-sustaining nuclear chain reaction
nodes.description: Enrico Fermi's Chicago Pile-1 produced the first controlled, self-sustaining fission reaction. The technical core of nuclear weapons and reactors.

nodes.label: Von Neumann-Morgenstern game theory
nodes.description: Theory of Games and Economic Behavior axiomatized strategic interaction and expected-utility decision. Created game theory as a discipline used across economics, biology, and political science.

nodes.label: Von Neumann stored-program architecture
nodes.description: The EDVAC report described a single-memory machine that stores data and instructions identically. The default architecture of digital computers ever since.

nodes.label: ENIAC general-purpose electronic computer
nodes.description: Mauchly and Eckert's room-sized vacuum-tube computer ran ballistic calculations a thousand times faster than electromechanical predecessors. The opening machine of the digital era.

nodes.label: Radiocarbon dating
nodes.description: Willard Libby exploited cosmogenic carbon-14 decay to date organic materials up to ~50,000 years old. Transformed archaeology, paleoecology, and Quaternary geology.

nodes.label: Arendt on totalitarianism
nodes.description: Hannah Arendt's Origins of Totalitarianism analyzed the convergence of imperialism, antisemitism, and mass politics into 20th-century total domination. A landmark of political theory.

nodes.label: Arrow's impossibility theorem
nodes.description: Kenneth Arrow proved that no aggregation rule can satisfy a small set of reasonable fairness conditions for ranked-choice voting. Founded modern social-choice theory.

nodes.label: Quine's Two Dogmas of Empiricism
nodes.description: Quine attacked the analytic-synthetic distinction and reductionism, arguing for a holistic, naturalized epistemology. Reset analytic philosophy of language and science.

nodes.label: Skinner's operant conditioning
nodes.description: B.F. Skinner systematized reinforcement-driven shaping of voluntary behavior. Underwrote a generation of behaviorist psychology, programmed instruction, and applied behavior analysis.

nodes.label: Salk polio vaccine
nodes.description: Jonas Salk's inactivated-virus vaccine, swiftly followed by Sabin's oral live vaccine, drove polio toward elimination across most of the world. A defining mid-20th-century public-health victory.

nodes.label: Levi-Strauss' structural anthropology
nodes.description: Claude Levi-Strauss applied Saussurean structuralism to kinship and myth, arguing that human cultural diversity is generated by a small number of underlying binary oppositions.

nodes.label: Chomsky's generative grammar
nodes.description: Syntactic Structures argued that human languages share a deep generative grammar - a hardwired cognitive faculty. Reframed linguistics, influenced AI, and inspired a behaviorism-vs-cognitivism realignment.

nodes.label: Sputnik 1, the first satellite
nodes.description: The Soviet Union placed the first artificial satellite in orbit. Triggered the space age, the US-Soviet space race, and the founding of NASA and ARPA.

nodes.label: FORTRAN high-level programming
nodes.description: John Backus' team at IBM produced the first widely used compiled high-level language. Made scientific computing accessible to non-specialists and established the compiler as a fundamental tool.

nodes.label: Keeling Curve of atmospheric CO2
nodes.description: Charles Keeling began continuous high-precision CO2 measurements at Mauna Loa, producing the rising sawtooth that became the iconic record of anthropogenic climate change.

nodes.label: LISP and AI programming
nodes.description: John McCarthy's LISP introduced symbolic computing, recursion, and garbage collection - and became the lingua franca of AI research for decades. Coined the term 'artificial intelligence.'

nodes.label: Integrated circuit
nodes.description: Jack Kilby and Robert Noyce independently demonstrated multiple components etched on a single semiconductor substrate. The economic substrate of Moore's law and the modern computing economy.

nodes.label: Oral contraceptive pill
nodes.description: FDA approval of Enovid made hormonal contraception widely accessible. Reshaped women's labor-force participation, family structure, and demographic trajectories worldwide.

nodes.label: Jacob and Monod's operon model
nodes.description: Francois Jacob and Jacques Monod showed that gene expression is regulated by repressor proteins binding DNA. The first molecular account of how cells turn genes on and off.

nodes.label: Milgram obedience experiment
nodes.description: Stanley Milgram's experiments showed ordinary subjects would administer apparently dangerous shocks under authority instruction. Reshaped social psychology and ethics of human-subjects research.

nodes.label: Kuhn's Structure of Scientific Revolutions
nodes.description: Thomas Kuhn argued that science alternates between long stretches of normal puzzle-solving and abrupt paradigm shifts. Coined a vocabulary that runs through every later discussion of scientific change.

nodes.label: Carson's Silent Spring
nodes.description: Rachel Carson's argument linking pesticides to ecosystem collapse helped launch the modern environmental movement and inspired the formation of agencies like the US EPA.

nodes.label: Cosmic microwave background
nodes.description: Penzias and Wilson serendipitously detected isotropic 2.7 K microwave radiation from every direction. The leftover heat of the Big Bang and the empirical pillar of modern cosmology.

nodes.label: Foucault on knowledge and power
nodes.description: Michel Foucault's Order of Things and later genealogies traced how knowledge regimes co-construct power. Reshaped history, sociology, and theory across the humanities.

nodes.label: First human heart transplant
nodes.description: Christiaan Barnard transplanted a heart from a donor into Louis Washkansky in Cape Town. Solid-organ transplantation became routine within decades.

nodes.label: Apollo 11 lunar landing
nodes.description: Armstrong and Aldrin walked on the Moon - the most ambitious civilian engineering project ever completed. Catalyzed integrated-circuit electronics, real-time computing, and materials science.

nodes.label: Unix operating system
nodes.description: Thompson and Ritchie at Bell Labs built a portable, multi-user OS organized around small composable utilities. Direct ancestor of Linux, macOS, and most modern server software.

nodes.label: Codd's relational data model
nodes.description: Edgar Codd proposed organizing data as mathematical relations queried in a declarative algebra. The conceptual basis of every modern relational database.

nodes.label: Low-loss optical fiber communications
nodes.description: Corning produced silica fiber with attenuation under 20 dB per kilometer, making long-haul optical telecom practical. The physical backbone of the global internet.

nodes.label: Microprocessor (Intel 4004)
nodes.description: Intel packed a complete CPU on a single silicon die. Within a decade microprocessors would be in cars, washing machines, and the first personal computers.

nodes.label: Networked email
nodes.description: Ray Tomlinson sent the first networked email between separate hosts on ARPANET, choosing '@' to separate user and host. Email quickly became the network's killer application.

nodes.label: Computed tomography (CT) scan
nodes.description: Godfrey Hounsfield's first clinical CT scanner reconstructed cross-sectional anatomy from X-ray projections. Inaugurated the era of digital cross-sectional medical imaging.

nodes.label: Ethernet local-area networking
nodes.description: Bob Metcalfe at Xerox PARC designed Ethernet's contention-based, carrier-sensing scheme for local networks. Became the dominant wired LAN protocol within a decade.

nodes.label: Public-key cryptography
nodes.description: Diffie and Hellman introduced asymmetric cryptography, with separate public and private keys. Made encrypted communication between strangers practical at internet scale.

nodes.label: Personal computer (Apple II era)
nodes.description: Apple II, Commodore PET, and TRS-80 made desktop computers consumer products. Opened markets that would dwarf the mainframe industry within a decade.

nodes.label: In vitro fertilization (Louise Brown)
nodes.description: Edwards and Steptoe achieved the first live human birth from in vitro fertilization. Created the field of assisted reproduction and reshaped family planning.

nodes.label: Kahneman-Tversky prospect theory
nodes.description: Daniel Kahneman and Amos Tversky modeled how real people deviate from expected-utility decisions, anchoring on reference points and weighting losses more than gains. Founded behavioral economics.

nodes.label: Smallpox eradicated
nodes.description: WHO declared smallpox the first disease eliminated from the wild. A decade-long campaign of ring vaccination made a once universal killer extinct in human populations.

nodes.label: Chicxulub impact and dinosaur extinction
nodes.description: Walter and Luis Alvarez identified anomalous iridium at the K-Pg boundary as evidence of an asteroid impact. Confirmed by the Chicxulub crater - reframed mass extinctions as catastrophic events.

nodes.label: Cosmic inflation
nodes.description: Alan Guth proposed that the very early universe underwent exponential expansion, smoothing it and seeding density fluctuations that grew into structure. The dominant - if still tested - early-universe paradigm.

nodes.label: TCP/IP transition
nodes.description: ARPANET completed its switch to TCP/IP, the protocol stack that would let arbitrary networks federate into the open Internet. Opened the era of public, permissionless networking.

nodes.label: Polymerase chain reaction (PCR)
nodes.description: Kary Mullis devised a thermocycling reaction that exponentially amplifies a chosen DNA sequence. Made molecular biology, forensics, and clinical genetics inexpensive and routine.

nodes.label: HIV identified as cause of AIDS
nodes.description: Montagnier's lab at Pasteur, with Gallo, isolated HIV-1 as the retrovirus underlying AIDS. Reframed a mysterious epidemic as a treatable infectious disease and seeded modern antiretroviral therapy.

nodes.label: DNA fingerprinting
nodes.description: Alec Jeffreys' minisatellite-based identification gave each individual a probabilistically unique DNA profile. Revolutionized forensics, paternity testing, and population genetics.

nodes.label: Helicobacter pylori causes ulcers
nodes.description: Marshall and Warren showed (Marshall self-experimenting) that a bacterium causes most peptic ulcers, replacing chronic acid-suppression with antibiotic cures. A vivid lesson on overlooked etiologies.

nodes.label: Stereolithography and 3D printing
nodes.description: Chuck Hull patented stereolithography, building 3D objects layer by layer from photo-cured resin. Inaugurated additive manufacturing, now standard in prototyping, dental and orthopedic devices, and aerospace.

nodes.label: Antarctic ozone hole
nodes.description: Joe Farman's team at the British Antarctic Survey detected the springtime Antarctic ozone collapse. Within two years the Montreal Protocol began phasing out CFCs - the model international response to a global pollutant.

nodes.label: Buckminsterfullerene (C60)
nodes.description: Kroto, Curl, and Smalley discovered a soccer-ball-shaped carbon allotrope. Opened nanocarbon chemistry, paving the way to nanotubes and graphene.

nodes.label: Sen's capability approach
nodes.description: Amartya Sen reframed welfare around what people are substantively able to do and be, not just income. Underwrote the UN Human Development Index and modern development economics.

nodes.label: World Wide Web
nodes.description: Tim Berners-Lee proposed and implemented HTTP, HTML, and URLs at CERN. Within a decade the Web was the dominant interface to the Internet.

nodes.label: First gene therapy clinical trial
nodes.description: Treatment of ADA-SCID with retroviral gene transfer in Ashanti DeSilva. Halting progress for two decades; routine clinical use of gene and cell therapies emerged in the 2010s and 2020s.

nodes.label: Functional MRI
nodes.description: Seiji Ogawa demonstrated that blood-oxygenation contrast (BOLD) lets MRI image neural activity. The dominant tool of human cognitive neuroscience for the next three decades.

nodes.label: IPCC First Assessment Report
nodes.description: The Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change synthesized the case that human activity is warming the planet. Anchored international climate negotiations from Rio to Paris.

nodes.label: Linux and open-source software
